name: Zenefits
summary: HR/benefits admin platform reached a $4.5B valuation but collapsed after regulators found employees using a script to bypass state insurance licensing training; the CEO was ousted and the company was later sold to Everstage/PeopleReign era buyers for a fraction of its peak value.
lesson: Growth built on regulatory shortcuts collapses fast once compliance failures surface publicly.
source: TechCrunch, Bloomberg reporting on Zenefits licensing scandal 2016

name: Doostang
summary: Invite-only social recruiting network for elite university/MBA job seekers failed to scale beyond a niche audience and shut down as LinkedIn absorbed professional networking.
lesson: A defensible niche can be erased when a horizontal platform (LinkedIn) expands into it.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Doostang shutdown

name: Jobster
summary: Recruitment advertising and social-recruiting startup burned through large VC rounds without finding a sustainable model and folded during the 2008 downturn.
lesson: Heavy ad spend to acquire employer customers without proven retention is unsustainable through a downturn.
source: GeekWire/TechCrunch Jobster shutdown coverage

name: Jobfox
summary: Matching-based job search startup aimed to replace resume keyword search with compatibility scoring but never gained enough employer adoption and wound down.
lesson: A better matching algorithm doesn't win if it can't get both sides of the marketplace to switch behavior.
source: Washington Post/TechCrunch Jobfox coverage

name: BranchOut
summary: Facebook-based professional networking and recruiting app grew virally to millions of users but engagement collapsed once Facebook throttled platform distribution, and it shut down.
lesson: Building growth entirely on another platform's distribution graph is fragile the moment that platform changes policy.
source: TechCrunch BranchOut shutdown reporting

name: Identified
summary: Social-data recruiting analytics startup was acquired by Workday in 2014 for roughly $175M but its consumer-facing product was shut down and never integrated as a standalone offering.
lesson: An acquihire outcome can still represent failure of the original product vision even when the exit price looks attractive.
source: TechCrunch Workday acquires Identified 2014

name: Poachable
summary: Anonymous candidate-matching recruiting startup out of a Silicon Valley accelerator shut down after failing to reach scale in a crowded recruiting-marketplace field.
lesson: Anonymized matching removes signal recruiters rely on, making it a hard sell against incumbents.
source: TechCrunch Poachable coverage

name: TalentBin
summary: Technical-talent sourcing search engine was acquired by Monster Worldwide in 2014 for about $17M, then largely discontinued as Monster itself declined and was absorbed by Randstad.
lesson: Being acquired by a struggling incumbent can be as fatal to a product as running out of runway independently.
source: TechCrunch Monster acquires TalentBin 2014

name: WhiteTruffle
summary: Algorithmic matching site for engineers and startups shut down after failing to reach the scale needed to sustain a two-sided marketplace.
lesson: Small seed-stage marketplaces in recruiting need a wedge beyond 'better matching' to survive incumbent competition.
source: TechCrunch WhiteTruffle shutdown coverage

name: Switch
summary: Swipe-based 'Tinder for jobs' mobile recruiting app generated press buzz but could not convert casual swiping into real hiring outcomes and shut down.
lesson: Consumer-app mechanics don't automatically translate into a functioning hiring marketplace.
source: Business Insider coverage of Switch app shutdown

name: Jobr
summary: Mobile swipe-to-apply job search app was acquired by Monster Worldwide in 2016 and was shut down within a couple of years as Monster consolidated products ahead of its Randstad sale.
lesson: Acquisitions by companies in decline often function as slow shutdowns rather than growth accelerants.
source: TechCrunch Monster acquires Jobr 2016

name: HireArt
summary: NYC-based recruiting marketplace connecting vetted candidates with employers wound down operations after failing to scale its high-touch vetting model profitably.
lesson: High-touch human vetting in a marketplace is expensive to scale and margins can stay thin indefinitely.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of HireArt closure

name: Zao
summary: AI-based recruiting chatbot startup failed to differentiate in an increasingly crowded conversational-recruiting space and shut down.
lesson: Conversational-AI recruiting tools need a clear ROI story beyond novelty chat UX to retain enterprise buyers.
source: Crunchbase/industry press on Zao shutdown

name: XOR
summary: AI recruiting-chatbot company that raised significant venture funding collapsed in 2023 amid allegations of falsified revenue figures to investors, leaving employees unpaid.
lesson: Misrepresenting revenue to raise capital delays but does not prevent collapse once cash runs out.
source: Business Insider/TechCrunch reporting on XOR 2023 shutdown

name: Triplebyte
summary: Technical-skills-assessment recruiting marketplace for engineers wound down and sold parts of its business in 2022 after a major pivot to a subscription model failed to reverse falling revenue.
lesson: A late-stage business-model pivot can accelerate decline if it alienates the customer base that generated the original revenue.
source: TechCrunch Triplebyte winding down 2022

name: Reflektive
summary: Real-time performance management platform that once raised at a unicorn-adjacent valuation ran out of funding and shut down operations in 2022 after failed acquisition talks.
lesson: Being an early mover in a hot HR-tech category doesn't protect against being outlasted once well-funded competitors saturate the market.
source: TechCrunch/HR Dive reporting on Reflektive shutdown 2022

name: Zugata
summary: AI-driven continuous performance-feedback startup rebranded and pivoted multiple times before quietly shutting down as larger platforms like Culture Amp and Lattice captured the market.
lesson: Repeated rebrand-and-pivot cycles signal an unresolved product-market-fit problem rather than solve it.
source: Crunchbase/industry coverage of Zugata

name: TinyPulse
summary: Early employee-engagement survey pioneer wound down its independent operations in 2023 after being outpaced by better-funded competitors such as Culture Amp and Lattice.
lesson: First-mover advantage in engagement surveys eroded once heavily funded platforms bundled the same features into broader HR suites.
source: HR Dive reporting on TinyPulse shutdown 2023

name: Maxwell Health
summary: Benefits administration and HR platform for small businesses was acquired by Sun Life Financial in 2018 for a fraction of its raised capital and was later folded into Sun Life's own tools.
lesson: Selling to a strategic acquirer at a steep discount is often the last resort before an outright shutdown.
source: Boston Business Journal/TechCrunch on Sun Life-Maxwell Health deal 2018

name: Benefitfocus
summary: Public benefits-administration software company struggled for years with unprofitability and was acquired by Voya Financial in 2023 for about $570M, well below its earlier market capitalization peak.
lesson: Chronic unprofitability as a public company eventually forces a distressed sale even for an established category leader.
source: Reuters coverage of Voya-Benefitfocus acquisition 2023

name: SmashFly
summary: Recruitment marketing and CRM platform was acquired by Symphony Talent in 2020 after years of competing unsuccessfully against Beamery, Phenom People, and SmartRecruiters, and its brand was retired.
lesson: Recruitment-marketing software became commoditized quickly, squeezing standalone vendors that lacked a broader platform.
source: HR Technologist coverage of Symphony Talent-SmashFly merger 2020

name: Symphony Talent
summary: Talent-marketing and CRM platform formed from merging SmashFly and other assets struggled to gain traction against Phenom and Beamery and effectively wound down its independent operations by 2023.
lesson: Rolling up struggling assets under private equity doesn't fix an underlying competitive-positioning problem.
source: Industry trade press on Symphony Talent decline

name: RolePoint
summary: Employee-referral and recruiting-analytics software company was acquired by SmashFly in 2017 and discontinued as a standalone product shortly after.
lesson: A feature-level product (referrals) can struggle to remain independent once platform vendors bundle the same capability.
source: TechCrunch SmashFly acquires RolePoint 2017

name: Koru
summary: Predictive hiring-assessment startup that measured 'soft skills' fit was acquired by mthree in 2021 for its technology and its independent product was discontinued.
lesson: Assessment startups selling a proprietary scoring methodology face a long, expensive sales cycle to prove ROI to enterprise HR buyers.
source: GeekWire coverage of mthree acquiring Koru 2021

name: WayUp
summary: Early-career and college recruiting marketplace, once valued highly with backing from major VCs, shut down in 2023 after failing to build a durable revenue model post-pandemic.
lesson: Marketplaces reliant on campus recruiting events are highly exposed to sudden shifts in employer hiring budgets.
source: TechCrunch reporting on WayUp shutdown 2023

name: Otta
summary: London-based tech-job-matching platform for startups was acquired by Kandidate/folded into a competitor in 2024 after struggling to scale its curated-matching model amid a slower tech hiring market.
lesson: Curated tech-hiring marketplaces are highly cyclical and vulnerable when the broader tech job market contracts.
source: Sifted/UK tech press coverage of Otta 2024

name: Belong
summary: AI-powered recruiting platform aiming to give candidates more control over the hiring process shut down in 2020 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Candidate-centric recruiting models struggle when the paying customer (the employer) is not the one being served first.
source: Sifted/TechCrunch coverage of Belong shutdown

name: TalentSky
summary: Freelance-talent-network and recruiting platform failed to gain traction against larger freelance marketplaces and shut down in 2016.
lesson: Entering a freelance-marketplace category already dominated by Upwork-scale incumbents leaves little room for a small entrant.
source: Crunchbase records on TalentSky closure

name: Wonolo
summary: On-demand staffing marketplace backed by Coca-Cola and major VCs shut down in 2023 after failing to reach profitability amid rising costs and softening gig-labor demand.
lesson: On-demand labor marketplaces require extremely tight unit economics that are hard to sustain through a funding downturn.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Wonolo shutdown 2023

name: Shiftgig
summary: On-demand staffing marketplace for hourly workers sold off its core assets in 2020 after years of failing to achieve profitable scale against Wonolo and Instawork.
lesson: Multiple well-funded competitors chasing the same hourly-staffing niche compress margins for everyone until most fail.
source: Chicago Tribune/TechCrunch coverage of Shiftgig sale 2020

name: HotJobs
summary: Once a top-three online job board, HotJobs was bought by Yahoo in 2002, then sold to Monster Worldwide in 2010 and folded into Monster's declining job-board business, effectively ending as a brand.
lesson: Legacy job boards were structurally vulnerable once search-driven aggregators like Indeed changed how candidates found jobs.
source: AdAge/press coverage of Yahoo-HotJobs-Monster transactions

name: CareerBuilder
funding: Majority owned by Apollo Global Management since 2017
summary: Once one of the largest US job boards, CareerBuilder saw traffic and revenue decline sharply as Indeed and LinkedIn captured job-search demand, leading to majority ownership passing to private equity at a steep discount to its former valuation.
lesson: Classified-style job boards lost to search-and-aggregation models that offered better candidate experience at lower employer cost.
source: Chicago Tribune/press coverage of Apollo acquiring CareerBuilder 2017

name: Monster Worldwide
summary: The pioneering online job board Monster.com was sold to Dutch staffing firm Randstad in 2016 for about $429M, a steep decline from its dot-com-era peak valuation above $8B.
lesson: Even category-defining pioneers can be dismantled by more efficient search-based successors like Indeed and LinkedIn.
source: Reuters coverage of Randstad-Monster Worldwide acquisition 2016

name: Namely
summary: HR and payroll platform for mid-sized companies raised over $260M at a high valuation but conducted repeated rounds of layoffs starting in 2019 and shrank significantly as it failed to reach profitable scale against Rippling, Gusto, and TriNet.
lesson: Raising at aggressive valuations without a path to profitability leaves a company exposed once investor sentiment shifts.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Namely layoffs 2019-2020

name: Hired.com
summary: Tech-talent recruiting marketplace once valued near $400M was acquired by Adecco-owned Vettery in 2020 at a steep markdown after failing to sustain growth and profitability.
lesson: Marketplace startups that scale headcount ahead of durable unit economics often end up sold to a rival at a fraction of peak valuation.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Adecco/Vettery acquiring Hired 2020

name: Weave (recruiting)
summary: Referral-based recruiting network startup failed to reach scale and shut down in 2018 after struggling to convert its social-graph approach into paying employer customers.
lesson: Social-graph recruiting products need a clear paid-conversion path, not just network growth, to survive.
source: Crunchbase records on Weave recruiting startup closure

name: Jobvite
summary: Early applicant-tracking-system leader was sold to private equity firm K1 Investment Management in 2018 at a valuation well below prior funding rounds, then merged into Employ Inc, losing its standalone identity as newer ATS vendors like Greenhouse and Lever captured market share.
lesson: Being an early ATS category leader doesn't guarantee survival once better-designed, venture-backed challengers emerge.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of K1 acquiring Jobvite 2018

name: TheLadders
summary: Premium job-matching service for high-salary professionals faced years of controversy over aggressive marketing and deceptive practices, leading to mass layoffs and a dramatic downsizing of its business model by the mid-2010s.
lesson: Aggressive, trust-eroding marketing tactics can permanently damage a recruiting brand's reputation and shrink its addressable market.
source: Forbes/press investigations into TheLadders marketing practices

name: Even (earned wage access)
summary: Earned-wage-access and financial wellness startup was acquired by Walmart in 2019 to become Even Responsible Finance, but Walmart shut the standalone brand down in 2023 in favor of other benefits partners.
lesson: Being acquired by a single anchor customer concentrates existential risk in that one relationship.
source: TechCrunch/Fortune coverage of Walmart discontinuing Even 2023

name: Poached
summary: Restaurant and hospitality hourly-hiring marketplace shut down in 2023 after failing to scale profitably against larger hourly-work platforms like Instawork and Snagajob.
lesson: Vertical-specific hourly-hiring marketplaces face intense pressure once horizontal platforms enter their niche with more capital.
source: Restaurant/hospitality trade press on Poached shutdown

name: Entelo
summary: AI-driven candidate-sourcing platform raised significant venture funding but wound down and sold its assets in 2023 after failing to differentiate against a growing field of AI sourcing tools.
lesson: Sourcing-automation tools face constant commoditization risk as AI capabilities become table stakes across the ATS ecosystem.
source: HR tech trade press coverage of Entelo winding down 2023

name: Gild
summary: Data-driven technical-recruiting platform was acquired by Ceridian's CEB unit (via Citadel-adjacent capital) in 2015 for about $85M, but its product was largely discontinued within a couple of years as the acquirer restructured.
lesson: An attractive acquisition price does not guarantee the acquired product survives internal restructuring at the buyer.
source: TechCrunch coverage of CEB acquiring Gild 2015

name: Workday
funding: IPO 2012, market cap tens of billions
summary: Cloud-based HCM and financial-management software company built by PeopleSoft founders became a dominant enterprise HR platform, growing into a top public SaaS company.
lesson: Founders with deep domain credibility and enterprise relationships can displace legacy on-premise incumbents with a cloud-native rebuild.
source: Workday SEC filings and public market data

name: ADP
summary: Automatic Data Processing built the dominant global payroll and HR outsourcing business over decades, continually modernizing into cloud HCM while maintaining market leadership.
lesson: Sustained reinvestment in a legacy category can preserve market leadership even as newer cloud entrants emerge.
source: ADP public financial filings

name: LinkedIn
summary: Professional networking platform became the dominant recruiting and talent-sourcing tool globally, culminating in Microsoft's $26.2B acquisition in 2016.
lesson: Owning the professional identity graph creates a durable network-effects moat that recruiting-point-solutions can't easily replicate.
source: Microsoft/LinkedIn acquisition press release 2016

name: Indeed
summary: Job-search engine built on aggregating listings across the web became the world's most-visited job site, acquired by Japan's Recruit Holdings and grown into a multi-billion-dollar business.
lesson: A search-first, candidate-focused UX beat classified-style job boards by directly optimizing for what job seekers actually wanted.
source: Recruit Holdings investor disclosures on Indeed acquisition

name: Glassdoor
summary: Employer-review and salary-transparency platform built a defensible data moat from user-generated content and was acquired by Recruit Holdings for $1.2B in 2018.
lesson: User-generated review content creates compounding data advantages that are hard for competitors to replicate quickly.
source: Recruit Holdings press release on Glassdoor acquisition 2018

name: ZipRecruiter
summary: Job-distribution and matching platform for SMB employers grew to a leading US recruiting-marketplace position and completed a direct listing on NYSE in 2021.
lesson: Focusing on SMB employers underserved by enterprise ATS vendors created a large, defensible market segment.
source: ZipRecruiter SEC S-1 filing and direct listing coverage 2021

name: Gusto
summary: Payroll, benefits, and HR platform for small businesses (originally ZenPayroll) scaled into a market leader serving hundreds of thousands of businesses with strong venture backing.
lesson: Simplifying a notoriously painful compliance workflow (payroll) for small businesses builds durable, sticky revenue.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of Gusto funding rounds

name: Rippling
summary: Unified HR, IT, and payroll platform founded by Parker Conrad became one of the fastest-growing HR-tech companies, reaching a multi-billion-dollar valuation by unifying employee systems of record.
lesson: Unifying historically siloed HR, IT, and finance workflows around one employee record creates strong platform lock-in.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Rippling funding rounds

name: Deel
summary: Global payroll and compliance platform for remote and international teams grew extremely quickly by simplifying cross-border employment and contractor payments.
lesson: The shift to remote/global work created urgent demand for compliant cross-border payroll infrastructure that Deel captured early.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Deel funding rounds

name: Checkr
summary: API-driven background-check platform modernized a slow, compliance-heavy industry and became a leading provider for gig-economy and enterprise employers alike.
lesson: Turning a compliance-heavy legacy service into a fast API unlocks new customer segments (gig platforms) the old players couldn't serve.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of Checkr funding rounds

name: TriNet
summary: Professional employer organization (PEO) providing outsourced HR, benefits, and payroll for SMBs built a durable, profitable public business serving hundreds of thousands of worksite employees.
lesson: The PEO co-employment model creates recurring, sticky revenue by bundling compliance risk transfer with HR services.
source: TriNet SEC filings

name: Paycor
summary: HR and payroll software provider focused on mid-market employers scaled steadily for decades and completed a public offering in 2021.
lesson: Decades of patient, focused execution in a specific customer segment (mid-market) can culminate in a successful IPO.
source: Paycor SEC S-1 filing 2021

name: Paylocity
summary: Cloud payroll and HCM software provider grew into a multi-billion-dollar public company by combining strong customer service with modern SaaS delivery for mid-sized employers.
lesson: Strong service quality alongside cloud software can be a durable differentiator against larger, less personal competitors.
source: Paylocity SEC filings

name: Paycom
summary: Single-database HCM and payroll software company grew into a highly profitable, multi-billion-dollar public company by emphasizing self-service employee data entry to reduce HR administrative burden.
lesson: A genuinely differentiated technical architecture (single database) can be a durable moat even in a mature software category.
source: Paycom SEC filings

name: Ceridian (Dayforce)
summary: Legacy payroll processor reinvented itself around its cloud Dayforce HCM platform, successfully transitioning from a declining outsourcing business to a growing SaaS company and later rebranding entirely as Dayforce.
lesson: An old-line services company can survive disruption by rebuilding its core product as cloud-native software rather than defending the legacy business.
source: Ceridian/Dayforce SEC filings

name: Ultimate Software (UKG)
funding: Acquired by Hellman & Friedman 2019 for $11B; merged with Kronos to form UKG
summary: Cloud HCM software provider was taken private in a record 2019 leveraged buyout, then merged with workforce-management leader Kronos to form UKG, one of the largest private HR-tech companies globally.
lesson: Merging complementary HCM and workforce-management leaders can create a combined platform stronger than either standalone company.
source: Press coverage of Hellman & Friedman-Ultimate Software LBO and Kronos merger 2019-2020

name: iCIMS
funding: Backed by Vista Equity Partners since 2021 at ~$1.5B+ valuation
summary: Enterprise applicant-tracking and talent-acquisition platform grew into a market leader serving thousands of large employers, remaining a top private HR-tech company under private equity ownership.
lesson: Focusing on deep enterprise ATS functionality can sustain leadership even as smaller point-solution ATS vendors proliferate.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of Vista Equity investment in iCIMS 2021

name: Greenhouse
summary: Structured-hiring applicant-tracking-system became a leading ATS for high-growth and enterprise companies, emphasizing hiring-process rigor and interview-kit standardization.
lesson: Selling a hiring methodology, not just software, differentiated Greenhouse from feature-comparable ATS competitors.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Greenhouse funding and TPG investment

name: SmartRecruiters
summary: Enterprise talent-acquisition suite grew into a global ATS/marketplace leader by combining recruiting software with an integrated recruiting-vendor marketplace.
lesson: Bundling a vendor marketplace inside the ATS creates additional revenue streams and switching costs beyond core recruiting software.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of SmartRecruiters funding rounds

name: BambooHR
summary: HR information system for small and mid-sized businesses grew profitably to serve tens of thousands of companies, becoming a category leader without heavy reliance on venture-scale burn.
lesson: A capital-efficient growth path focused on real profitability can outcompete better-funded rivals in the SMB HR software category.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of BambooHR growth

name: Justworks
summary: PEO platform providing payroll, benefits, and compliance for small businesses grew into a leading modern PEO and completed a public listing, differentiating on simplicity and customer support.
lesson: A modernized, transparent take on the PEO model can win share from legacy providers by prioritizing customer experience.
source: Justworks SEC S-1 filing and IPO coverage 2025

name: Sterling Check
summary: Background-screening and identity-verification provider grew into one of the largest employment background-check companies globally and completed a NASDAQ IPO in 2021.
lesson: Consolidating a fragmented background-check industry through acquisitions can build scale advantages that support a successful public offering.
source: Sterling Check SEC S-1 filing 2021

name: Handshake
summary: Early-career recruiting platform connecting college students and employers built exclusive partnerships with universities, becoming the dominant campus-recruiting network in the US.
lesson: Securing exclusive institutional partnerships (university career centers) created a defensible network moat competitors couldn't easily replicate.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Handshake funding rounds

name: Doximity
summary: Professional network and recruiting platform for physicians and healthcare providers built a highly profitable business by combining networking, telehealth tools, and physician recruiting for health systems.
lesson: Building a vertical professional network for a high-value, underserved niche (physicians) can be more profitable than competing horizontally with LinkedIn.
source: Doximity SEC S-1 filing and IPO coverage 2021

name: Paradox
summary: Conversational AI recruiting assistant (Olivia) automating high-volume hourly hiring scaled rapidly to serve major retail and healthcare employers, reaching unicorn valuation.
lesson: Targeting high-volume, high-turnover hourly hiring with conversational AI addressed a pain point enterprise ATS vendors underserved.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Paradox funding rounds

name: Eightfold AI
summary: AI-powered talent-intelligence platform for enterprise workforce planning and internal mobility scaled to serve large global enterprises and reached unicorn status.
lesson: Applying deep-learning talent-matching to internal mobility, not just external hiring, expanded the addressable enterprise budget.
source: Crunchbase/press coverage of Eightfold AI funding rounds

name: SeekOut
summary: AI-powered talent-sourcing search engine for recruiters, founded by former Microsoft and Amazon engineers, grew quickly into a leading enterprise sourcing tool.
lesson: Deep technical search expertise applied to a narrow, well-understood recruiter workflow (sourcing) can scale fast with strong founder credibility.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of SeekOut funding rounds

name: Lattice
summary: Performance management and employee-engagement platform grew rapidly during the 'people ops' software wave, becoming a category leader alongside Culture Amp.
lesson: Riding the broader shift toward continuous performance management (away from annual reviews) drove fast category-defining growth.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Lattice funding rounds

name: Culture Amp
summary: Employee-engagement and performance-management platform grew from Australia into a global leader serving thousands of companies, reaching unicorn valuation.
lesson: Combining survey analytics with actionable people-science guidance differentiated Culture Amp from simple survey tools.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Culture Amp funding rounds

name: SuccessFactors
summary: Cloud-based talent-management software pioneer went public in 2007 and was acquired by SAP in 2011 for $3.4B, becoming the foundation of SAP's cloud HCM strategy.
lesson: Being an early cloud-native alternative to on-premise HR suites made SuccessFactors a natural, high-value acquisition target for legacy ERP vendors.
source: SAP press release on SuccessFactors acquisition 2011

name: Cornerstone OnDemand
funding: IPO 2011, taken private by Clearlake Capital 2021 for $5.2B
summary: Cloud learning and talent-management platform scaled to a public company serving thousands of enterprises before being taken private by Clearlake Capital in a $5.2B deal in 2021.
lesson: A durable enterprise learning-management niche can sustain decades of growth through both public-market and private-equity ownership phases.
source: Press coverage of Clearlake Capital-Cornerstone OnDemand acquisition 2021

name: Glint
summary: People-analytics and employee-engagement platform was acquired by LinkedIn (Microsoft) in 2018 for a reported $400M+ and integrated into LinkedIn's Talent Solutions suite.
lesson: Strong data-science-driven engagement analytics made Glint a natural fit for LinkedIn's broader talent-platform ambitions.
source: TechCrunch coverage of LinkedIn acquiring Glint 2018

name: Homebase
summary: Scheduling, time-tracking, and hiring platform for hourly small-business teams grew to serve well over 100,000 businesses, becoming a leading operations platform for the hourly workforce.
lesson: Bundling scheduling, payroll, and hiring for hourly small-business teams addressed a workflow no single point solution covered well.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Homebase funding rounds

name: Deputy
summary: Workforce-management platform for shift-based employees scaled globally from Australia to serve hundreds of thousands of workplaces, reaching unicorn valuation.
lesson: A focused, global-from-day-one strategy in shift-work scheduling let Deputy scale beyond its home market into a large addressable base.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of Deputy funding rounds
